text:
This study investigates the dynamic light absorption of wood-burning organic carbon (OC) produced under smoldering conditions and aged photochemically in a $104\text{ m}^3$ dual outdoor chamber under natural sunlight. Using a UV-visible spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere, we tracked the evolution of OC light absorption across varying relative humidity (RH) and $\text{NO}_x$ conditions. Our results reveal a complex diurnal pattern: in the morning, the mass absorption cross section (integrated from 280 to 600 nm) typically increased by 11–54%, suggesting the secondary formation of chromophores. However, as photooxidation progressed into the afternoon, sunlight bleaching became the dominant mechanism, causing the absorption to decay by 19–68%. 

Environmental conditions significantly influenced these optical transitions. High RH was found to accelerate the loss of color, likely due to aqueous-phase photochemistry, while the presence of $\text{NO}_x$ slightly sustained the aerosol’s light-absorbing properties by promoting the formation of nitrogen-containing chromophores. To connect these optical changes to chemical transformations, we employed various spectroscopic techniques. Fluorescence intensity, primarily associated with polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), dropped sharply over time, indicating PAH degradation. Additionally, a steady decline in levoglucosan concentrations confirmed the chemical aging of primary organic aerosol. Infrared spectroscopy further revealed that the wood-burning particles became less hygroscopic as they aged, despite becoming more oxygenated. These chamber findings closely mirror trends observed in ambient smoke from the 2012 County Line wildfire in Florida. We conclude that after roughly 8–9 hours of exposure to sunlight, biomass-burning OC becomes significantly less light-absorbing than its freshly emitted counterpart, a finding with important implications for atmospheric radiative forcing models.
